questions.question: Using the Duracher formula, what cuffed ET tube size is recommended for a 3-year-old child?
options: A. 3.5 mm
B. 4.0 mm
C. 4.5 mm
D. 5.0 mm
questions.rationale: Duracher formula: (age/4) + 3.5 = (3/4) + 3.5 = 0.75 + 3.5 = 4.25 mm. Rounded to the nearest 0.5 mm, this equals 4.0 mm. When the value falls exactly between two 0.5-mm increments, rounding down is the standard convention.

questions.question: Once an advanced airway (ET tube or SGA) has been placed and confirmed during pediatric CPR, how should compressions and ventilations be coordinated?
options: A. Pause compressions for every 2 breaths (30:2 ratio)
B. Continue compressions without interruption; deliver 1 breath every 2–3 seconds (20–30/min) independently
C. Pause compressions for every 3 breaths
D. Deliver 2 breaths after every 15 compressions
questions.rationale: With an advanced airway confirmed, compressions become continuous and ventilations are delivered independently at 1 breath every 2–3 seconds (20–30/min). This eliminates compression pauses and improves the compression fraction.

questions.question: A 5-year-old (18 kg) is in cardiac arrest. An advanced airway has been placed and confirmed. During CPR, the ETCO₂ reading is 7 mmHg. What does this indicate?
options: A. Excellent CPR quality
B. The ET tube is likely misplaced
C. Compression quality may be inadequate — reassess compression technique
D. The child has ROSC
questions.rationale: ETCO₂ below 10 mmHg during CPR suggests inadequate cardiac output from compressions. The team should reassess compression depth, rate, recoil, and hand position. A sustained waveform confirms the tube is in the correct position, but the low value points to poor compression quality.

questions.question: What does the 'P' in the DOPE mnemonic stand for?
options: A. Pneumonia
B. Pulmonary embolism
C. Pneumothorax
D. Pharyngeal obstruction
questions.rationale: DOPE stands for Displacement, Obstruction, Pneumothorax, and Equipment. Pneumothorax (especially tension pneumothorax from barotrauma) is a critical cause of sudden deterioration in a ventilated child and requires immediate recognition and treatment.

questions.question: Why are cuffed ET tubes now preferred over uncuffed tubes for most pediatric patients?
options: A. Cuffed tubes are smaller and easier to place
B. They provide more precise ventilation control, reduce aspiration risk, and improve ETCO₂ monitoring
C. Cuffed tubes eliminate the need for ETCO₂ confirmation
D. Uncuffed tubes are no longer available
questions.rationale: Cuffed tubes allow more precise control of ventilation volume and pressure, reduce the risk of aspiration around the tube, and provide a more reliable ETCO₂ waveform. They should be inflated to the minimum occlusive volume to prevent tracheal injury.

questions.question: What is the ventilation rate during CPR when an advanced airway is in place for an infant?
options: A. 10–12 breaths per minute
B. 20–30 breaths per minute (1 breath every 2–3 seconds)
C. 40–60 breaths per minute
D. Same as BLS: 2 breaths after every 15 or 30 compressions
questions.rationale: The ventilation rate with an advanced airway is 1 breath every 2–3 seconds (20–30 breaths per minute), regardless of whether the patient is an infant or a child. This is delivered without pausing compressions.

questions.question: A nurse is about to intubate a 7-year-old child (22 kg). Using the Duracher formula, what cuffed ET tube size should be selected?
options: A. 4.5 mm
B. 5.0 mm
C. 5.5 mm
D. 6.0 mm
questions.rationale: Duracher formula: (age/4) + 3.5 = (7/4) + 3.5 = 1.75 + 3.5 = 5.25 mm, rounded to the nearest 0.5 mm = 5.5 mm.

questions.question: Which of the following is the GOLD STANDARD for confirming correct ET tube placement in a patient with a perfusing rhythm?
options: A. Bilateral breath sounds on auscultation
B. Chest rise during ventilation
C. ETCO₂ waveform on capnography
D. Misting inside the ET tube
questions.rationale: A sustained, reliable ETCO₂ waveform is the gold standard for confirming tracheal tube placement in a patient with a perfusing rhythm. Breath sounds and chest rise are supportive but can be misleading (e.g., in obesity or thick chest walls).

questions.question: What is the most common cause of sudden ET tube displacement during pediatric resuscitation?
options: A. The tube was the wrong size
B. Movement of the child during resuscitation, especially during CPR or positioning
C. Equipment malfunction
D. Cuff over-inflation
questions.rationale: The most common cause of tube displacement is movement — during CPR, patient repositioning, or transport. Securing the tube firmly and reassessing placement after any movement are critical. The trachea in children is shorter and more mobile than in adults.

questions.question: Why is bag-mask ventilation technique particularly important in pediatric patients?
options: A. Children cannot tolerate an advanced airway
B. Children have a proportionally larger head, anterior larynx, and narrower airway, making ventilation more challenging and gastric insufflation more likely with poor technique
C. Bag-mask ventilation is only used in pediatric patients
D. Children need less ventilation than adults
questions.rationale: Pediatric anatomic differences (large head/occiput, anterior larynx, narrow subglottic space, compliant chest) make airway management more challenging. Poor technique with excessive ventilation or inadequate seal leads to gastric insufflation and ineffective oxygenation.

questions.question: A child with an ET tube suddenly loses the ETCO₂ waveform, and breath sounds are absent on the left side but present on the right. What is the MOST likely cause?
options: A. Pneumothorax
B. Equipment failure
C. Displacement — the tube has likely advanced into the right mainstem bronchus
D. Obstruction from a mucus plug
questions.rationale: Loss of the ETCO₂ waveform with absent left breath sounds and present right breath sounds is classic for mainstem (right bronchus) intubation — the tube has been pushed too far down, bypassing the left mainstem. This is a form of displacement requiring tube withdrawal to the correct depth.

questions.question: When is it appropriate to use a supraglottic airway device (SGA) as a rescue device during pediatric resuscitation?
options: A. As the first-line device for all pediatric patients
B. When a trained provider cannot achieve endotracheal intubation within a reasonable time frame
C. Only after cardiac arrest has lasted more than 15 minutes
D. SGAs are not recommended in pediatric patients
questions.rationale: An SGA (LMA, i-gel, or similar) is appropriate when ET intubation is unsuccessful or not immediately available. SGA placement is faster and requires less skill than ET intubation, making it a valuable rescue device in cardiac arrest.

questions.question: During CPR with an advanced airway, the team notices an abrupt increase in ETCO₂ from 8 mmHg to 35 mmHg. What does this most likely indicate?
options: A. The tube has become displaced
B. Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)
C. Equipment failure
D. Tension pneumothorax
questions.rationale: An abrupt, sustained rise in ETCO₂ during CPR is a strong indicator of ROSC. When the heart begins beating again, pulmonary blood flow increases dramatically, and CO₂ elimination rises. The team should immediately check for a pulse.

questions.question: Why should you avoid over-ventilating during bag-mask ventilation in a child?
options: A. Over-ventilation does not have significant consequences in children
B. Excessive rate or volume causes gastric insufflation, aspiration risk, and increased intrathoracic pressure that reduces venous return
C. Children have too much lung capacity to be over-ventilated
D. Over-ventilation only matters in neonates
questions.rationale: Over-ventilation pushes air into the stomach (gastric insufflation), increasing aspiration risk. It also raises intrathoracic pressure, which reduces venous return to the heart and decreases cardiac output — particularly harmful during CPR.

questions.question: An 8-year-old (25 kg) needs emergency intubation. Using the Duracher formula, what cuffed ET tube size is recommended?
options: A. 5.0 mm
B. 5.5 mm
C. 6.0 mm
D. 6.5 mm
questions.rationale: Duracher formula: (8/4) + 3.5 = 2.0 + 3.5 = 5.5 mm. Since 5.5 mm is already at a 0.5-mm increment, no rounding is needed. The correct cuffed ET tube size is 5.5 mm.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of ETCO₂ monitoring during cardiac arrest?
options: A. Confirming correct ET tube placement
B. Assessing CPR compression quality
C. Detecting ROSC
D. Measuring blood glucose levels
questions.rationale: ETCO₂ monitoring serves three critical roles: confirming tube placement (sustained waveform), assessing CPR quality (values < 10 mmHg suggest poor compressions), and detecting ROSC (abrupt rise). It does not measure blood glucose.

questions.question: A 2-year-old child with croup is being bag-mask ventilated in the emergency department. Which anatomic feature makes this child more difficult to ventilate than an adult?
options: A. Children have a smaller tongue relative to the oral cavity
B. The pediatric airway is proportionally narrower, especially at the cricoid cartilage, and the larynx is higher and more anterior
C. Children have thicker neck muscles that obstruct the airway
D. The epiglottis is flatter and wider in children
questions.rationale: The pediatric airway has a proportionally narrower lumen (especially at the cricoid cartilage), a higher and more anterior larynx, a U-shaped epiglottis, and a proportionally larger tongue — all of which make mask ventilation and intubation more challenging than in adults.

questions.question: What is the minimum occlusive volume for inflating an ET tube cuff?
options: A. The maximum volume listed on the cuff
B. Just enough air to prevent a leak during positive-pressure ventilation
C. 25 mL regardless of tube size
D. The cuff should not be inflated in pediatric patients
questions.rationale: The minimum occlusive volume is the smallest amount of air needed to prevent a leak during ventilation. Over-inflation beyond this point increases tracheal mucosal pressure, risking ischemia, edema, and post-extubation stridor.

questions.question: A child with an advanced airway suddenly develops hypotension, absent breath sounds on the right, and tracheal deviation to the left. Using DOPE, what is the most likely cause?
options: A. Displacement of the tube
B. Obstruction of the tube
C. Tension pneumothorax (Pneumothorax)
D. Equipment failure
questions.rationale: Absent breath sounds on one side, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, and hypotension are classic signs of tension pneumothorax — the 'P' in DOPE. This requires immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube placement.

questions.question: What is the key difference between ventilation during BLS and ventilation once an advanced airway is placed?
options: A. There is no difference — ventilations are always delivered with compressions paused

B. During BLS, ventilations are coordinated with compressions (30:2 or 15:2); with an advanced airway, compressions are continuous and ventilations are delivered independently at 1 breath every 2–3 seconds
C. Advanced airway ventilation requires a higher rate of 60 breaths per minute
D. BLS does not include any ventilations
questions.rationale: BLS uses compression-ventilation ratios (30:2 or 15:2) with pauses for breaths. Once an advanced airway is confirmed, compressions are delivered continuously and ventilations are given independently at 1 breath every 2–3 seconds (20–30/min), eliminating the need to pause compressions.
